one_line_takeaway: Applying vanilla FGSM to hydrological forcing inputs across 1,347 German catchments shows that a physical-conceptual model (HBV) is consistently more sensitive to small adversarial perturbations than a comparably trained LSTM, and both models' local performance degradation scales approximately linearly with perturbation magnitude -- a diagnostic-evaluation finding, not a new attack or defense.
key_finding: Under FGSM with eps=0.2, median KGE across 1,347 catchments drops from 0.833 to 0.728 for the LSTM (delta -0.105) and from 0.681 to 0.517 for HBV (delta -0.164); MSE increases at every single catchment for both models (by construction, since FGSM is computed on the MSE loss). Degradation is nearly universal: only 5/1347 (0.37%) LSTM catchments and 3/1347 (0.22%) HBV catchments show any improvement. Contrary to the authors' prior expectation that DL models would be less robust (motivated by adversarial-example literature in vision/NLP), the LSTM is consistently more robust than HBV -- confirmed after stratifying by baseline KGE (Table 1: e.g., for KGEo in [0.8,0.9], median KGE drop is 0.117/MSE increase 0.140 for HBV vs. 0.075/0.075 for LSTM; for KGEo in [0,0.5], HBV drop 0.220 vs. LSTM 0.115) and via median regression slopes across all 1,347 catchments (HBV: -0.753 for dKGE/eps, 0.843 for dMSE/eps; LSTM: -0.479 for dKGE/eps, 0.586 for dMSE/eps). Even after perturbation, the LSTM (median KGE 0.728) still outperforms the unperturbed HBV (median KGE 0.681). Both models show strongly linear local responses to perturbation magnitude: for an example catchment (DE911520), fitting Q_t(eps) per time step gives median R2 > 0.99 for both models, with ~90% of time steps at R2 >= 0.95; median |slope| is 0.160 (LSTM) vs. 0.265 (HBV) -- interpreted as evidence that within this small-perturbation neighborhood the LSTM's ReLU-MLP head stays in a fixed activation regime (sign-flip rate <3%) and HBV's piecewise-linear/affine state updates plus fixed linear unit-hydrograph routing keep the system near a first-order (locally affine) regime. This linearity generalizes beyond the FGSM gradient-sign direction: 100 random perturbation directions produce comparably high median R2 (~0.996 LSTM, ~0.998 HBV) and comparable slope magnitudes, indicating the local linearity is a general neighborhood property, not specific to the adversarial (gradient) direction. Sensitivity correlates with catchment aridity: for HBV, Spearman correlation between aridity index (PET/P) and MSE increase is 0.76 (KGE decrease: 0.47); for LSTM, 0.69 (MSE) and 0.43 (KGE).
core_contribution: The paper introduces adversarial robustness analysis as an evaluation methodology for rainfall-runoff (streamflow) hydrological models, comparing a physical-conceptual model (HBV, differentiable variant) against a regionally trained LSTM across 1,347 German catchments (CAMELS-DE). Using the Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) applied to meteorological forcing inputs (precipitation, temperature, potential evapotranspiration), the authors quantify how much predictive performance (KGE, MSE) degrades under small, gradient-based perturbations, and characterize whether that degradation is approximately linear in perturbation magnitude epsilon. They also probe internal model mechanisms (LSTM ReLU-MLP activation sign flips, LSTM hidden-channel activations, HBV's five conceptual storage compartments) to explain why near-linear responses emerge. This is presented as the first systematic study of adversarial robustness in rainfall-runoff modeling; it is an empirical evaluation/diagnostic study, not a proposal of a new attack or defense method (FGSM is used off-the-shelf from Goodfellow et al. 2014).
threat_model: The paper's own framing (Section 1.4) is explicitly security-motivated: it cites real, increasing cyber incidents against water infrastructure (Tuptuk et al., 2021) and a specific reported case in which adversaries gained reconnaissance access to a New York dam's control system that used temperature and water-level sensor readings (Hassanzadeh et al., 2020), noting such access could later enable stealthy, small-magnitude sensor manipulation that biases model-driven operational decisions. However, the paper does not implement or evaluate an attacker who compromises real sensors or a control loop; instead it treats FGSM-perturbed meteorological forcings as a proxy/worst-case stand-in for such input manipulation, and the 'attacker' capability modeled is white-box gradient access to a differentiable hydrological model, not a black-box or sensor-spoofing threat model. No defense mechanism is proposed or evaluated; robustness is only measured, not improved.
limitations: The authors explicitly state (Sections 5.3-5.4): (1) results pertain only to a single, specific HBV variant (Hy2DL-adapted) and a single LSTM configuration trained only on P/T/PET (no static catchment attributes) -- conclusions may not generalize to other HBV variants or DL architectures; (2) the study uses only single-step FGSM within a small eps range (0-0.5), and near-linearity is explicitly framed as a local, not global, property -- the authors note LSTMs are known to eventually saturate globally (citing Baste et al., 2025) and that any smooth nonlinear function looks locally linear near an operating point; (3) no catastrophic-failure or larger/iterative-attack regime is explored in depth (Basic Iterative Method results are only mentioned qualitatively, not shown); (4) the paper cannot determine what the 'correct' or ideal level of adversarial robustness is for a hydrological model, since a trivially robust model (always predicting zero flow) would be useless -- there is an acknowledged robustness/predictive-capability trade-off with no resolution offered; (5) it remains unknown how a real catchment (as opposed to a differentiable model of it) would actually respond to analogous real-world input perturbations, so the ecological/operational validity of the adversarial framing is explicitly left open; (6) no defense method is proposed or tested, only measurement of existing vulnerability.

datasets_used: CAMELS-DE v1.0.0 (Loritz et al., 2024) -- daily hydro-meteorological forcings, discharge, and catchment attributes for 1,582 German catchments, 1951-2020; 1,347 catchments retained after filtering for sufficient discharge data in each of the 1981-2000/2001-2010/2011-2020 splits

CAMELS-DE Caravan Extension (Caravan-DE, ERA5-Land-derived) used only as an independent reference product to estimate real-world meteorological measurement uncertainty for calibrating the adversarial perturbation magnitude eps
quantitative_results: Median KGE at FGSM eps=0.2: LSTM 0.728 (delta -0.105), HBV 0.517 (delta -0.164)
Catchments improving under attack: LSTM 5/1347 (0.37%), HBV 3/1347 (0.22%); MSE worsens at 100% of catchments for both models
Median across-catchment linear-fit slopes: HBV -0.753 (dKGE/eps), 0.843 (dMSE/eps); LSTM -0.479 (dKGE/eps), 0.586 (dMSE/eps)

Per-time-step linearity for example catchment DE911520 (Table 2): median R2 0.995 (LSTM) / 0.993 (HBV); mean R2 0.976/0.974; fraction of time steps with R2>=0.95: 0.899 (LSTM)/0.884 (HBV); median |slope| 0.160 (LSTM)/0.265 (HBV)

LSTM final training config: 1 LSTM layer, 254 hidden units, 16-dim catchment embedding, MLP 254->13(ReLU)->5(ReLU)->1; median test-period KGE 0.833 vs. 0.84 reported by Loritz et al. (2024) under different settings

Meteorological uncertainty basis for eps=0.2: HYRAS precipitation MAE ~1-2 mm/day vs gauges; example catchment DE911520 P MAE 1.38 mm/day (bias 0.09), T MAE 0.53C (bias -0.22C); driest catchment DEE10410 MAE/bias range 0.15-1.15 across variables
Spearman correlation between catchment aridity index (PET/P) and performance degradation: HBV 0.76 (MSE increase), 0.47 (KGE decrease); LSTM 0.69 (MSE increase), 0.43 (KGE decrease)
LSTM internal-mechanism checks: MLP ReLU pre-activation sign-flip rate <3% across tested eps; per-channel LSTM hidden-activation-vs-eps linear fits median R2 ~0.999, median |slope| 0.014
HBV internal-store linear fits vs eps for DE911520: R2 0.975-0.999 across the five stores (SNOWPACK, MELTWATER, SM, SUZ, SLZ); slopes ranging from -0.0725 (SNOWPACK) to 20.2 (SM)

Random-direction (non-gradient) linearity check, 100 directions, catchment DE911520: median R2 ~0.996 (LSTM) / ~0.998 (HBV); median |slope| ~0.036 (LSTM) / ~0.076 (HBV); fraction of time steps R2>=0.95 ~0.896 (LSTM) / ~0.881 (HBV)
baselines_compared: HBV (differentiable, process-based conceptual model, Hy2DL implementation) vs. LSTM (regionally trained deep learning model) -- the paper's own head-to-head comparison, both under identical FGSM attack settings and identical P/T/PET-only inputs

Referenced but not re-run in this paper: Lees et al. (2021) LSTM (NSE 0.88) vs. Sacramento process-based model (NSE 0.8) in UK gauged streamflow; Kratzert, Klotz, Herrnegger et al. (2019) LSTM ungauged vs. Sacramento gauged on CAMELS -- cited as prior motivating evidence, not part of this paper's own experiments

Referenced concurrent/related work (not integrated into this paper's own results): Xia et al. (2025) trustworthiness benchmarking of LSTM, Informer, and DeepONet architectures for water-quality prediction, cited as showing robustness rankings differ across DL architectures
